title: Basic Counting Principles
digitizedText:
# Chapter 11: Permutations and Combinations

## 11.1 Basic Counting Principles

In combinatorics, the fundamental counting principles help determine the number of possible outcomes for multiple events.

### The Addition Rule (The "OR" Rule)
If an event can occur in $m$ ways and a second, mutually exclusive event can occur in $n$ ways, then the total number of ways either of the events can occur is $m + n$.

### The Multiplication Rule (The "AND" Rule)
If a sequence of events consists of one event that can occur in $m$ ways, followed by a second independent event that can occur in $n$ ways, the total number of ways the sequence can occur is $m \times n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1: Applying Counting Principles**
> A restaurant offers $3$ starters, $5$ main courses, and $4$ desserts. How many different three-course meals can be chosen?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Identify the number of choices for each course: $3$ starters, $5$ mains, and $4$ desserts.
> 2. Since the choices are independent and we are choosing a starter AND a main AND a dessert, use the multiplication rule.
> 3. Calculate the total combinations: $3 \times 5 \times 4 = 60$.
> 4. There are $60$ possible three-course meals.

title: Factorials
digitizedText:
## 11.2 Factorials

A **factorial** represents the product of an integer and all the positive integers below it. It is denoted by an exclamation mark ($!$).

$$ n! = n \times (n - 1) \times (n - 2) \times \dots \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 $$

**Key Properties:**
*   $1! = 1$
*   $0! = 1$ (By mathematical convention, there is exactly $1$ way to arrange zero objects).
*   $n! = n \times (n - 1)!$

title: Permutations
digitizedText:
## 11.3 Permutations

A **permutation** is an arrangement of objects where the **order does matter**. Arranging a sequence of letters, digits to form a password, or people in specific seats are examples of permutations.

### Arranging $n$ Distinct Objects
The number of ways to arrange $n$ distinct objects in a line is $n!$.

### Arranging $r$ Objects from $n$ Distinct Objects
If you have $n$ distinct objects and want to arrange $r$ of them, the formula is:

$$ ^{n}P_{r} = \frac{n!}{(n - r)!} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 2: Simple Permutations**
> In a race with $8$ runners, in how many ways can the gold, silver, and bronze medals be awarded?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. We are selecting $3$ runners from $8$, and the order of medals matters (Gold $\neq$ Silver).
> 2. Use the permutation formula with $n = 8$ and $r = 3$:
>    $$ ^{8}P_{3} = \frac{8!}{(8 - 3)!} = \frac{8!}{5!} $$
> 3. Expand and cancel terms: $8 \times 7 \times 6 = 336$.
> 4. There are $336$ ways to award the medals.

title: Combinations
digitizedText:
## 11.4 Combinations

A **combination** is a selection of objects where the **order does NOT matter**. Choosing a committee, picking a team, or selecting a set of books are examples of combinations.

### Selecting $r$ Objects from $n$ Distinct Objects
The formula to select $r$ objects from $n$ distinct objects is:

$$ ^{n}C_{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n - r)!} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 3: Basic Selections**
> A team of $4$ students is to be chosen from a group of $10$ students. How many different teams can be formed?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Identify that order does not matter (a team is an unordered group), so use combinations.
> 2. Use the formula for $^{10}C_{4}$:
>    $$ ^{10}C_{4} = \frac{10!}{4!(10 - 4)!} = \frac{10!}{4! \times 6!} $$
> 3. Calculate: $\frac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} = 210$.
> 4. There are $210$ possible teams.

title: Arrangements with Restrictions
digitizedText:
## 11.5 Arrangements with Restrictions

Often, permutations come with specific constraints. Let us explore the common techniques to solve them.

### 1. Grouping Items Together (The "Tie" Method)
If certain items must sit or be grouped together, treat them as a single combined "super-unit".

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 4: Items Grouped Together**
> How many ways can the letters of the word $COMPUTER$ be arranged if the vowels ($O, U, E$) must always be next to each other?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Treat the $3$ vowels ($O, U, E$) as $1$ single unit.
> 2. Count the remaining units. There are $5$ consonants ($C, M, P, T, R$) plus $1$ vowel unit, totaling $6$ units.
> 3. Arrange the $6$ units: $6! = 720$ ways.
> 4. Arrange the $3$ vowels internally within their unit: $3! = 6$ ways.
> 5. Multiply the arrangements: $720 \times 6 = 4320$ ways.

### 2. Separating Items (The "Slot" Method)
If items must not sit together, arrange the unrestricted items first and place the restricted items into the gaps between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 5: Separating Items**
> How many ways can $4$ boys and $3$ girls be arranged in a line if no two girls can stand next to each other?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Arrange the boys first. There are $4$ boys, yielding $4! = 24$ ways.
> 2. Create "slots" around the boys for the girls to stand in: $\_ \ B \ \_ \ B \ \_ \ B \ \_ \ B \ \_$
> 3. There are $5$ available slots for the $3$ girls. The order matters, so we use $^{5}P_{3} = \frac{5!}{2!} = 60$ ways.
> 4. Multiply the independent arrangements: $24 \times 60 = 1440$ ways.

### 3. Permutations with Identical Objects
If you are arranging $n$ objects where some objects are indistinguishable (e.g., $n_1$ are alike, $n_2$ are alike, etc.), divide the total permutations by the factorials of the repeated objects.

$$ \text{Arrangements} = \frac{n!}{n_1! \times n_2! \times \dots \times n_k!} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 6: Indistinguishable Objects**
> Find the number of distinct arrangements of the letters in the word $MATHEMATICS$.
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Count the total number of letters: $n = 11$.
> 2. Identify the frequencies of repeated letters: $M$ appears $2$ times, $A$ appears $2$ times, and $T$ appears $2$ times.
> 3. Apply the formula:
>    $$ \frac{11!}{2! \times 2! \times 2!} $$
> 4. Calculate the result: $\frac{39916800}{8} = 4989600$ arrangements.

title: Selections with Restrictions
digitizedText:
## 11.6 Selections with Restrictions

Combinations can also have conditions, such as forcing the inclusion or exclusion of items, or selecting items from multiple distinct categories.

### 1. Multi-Category Selections
When forming a group from different categories (e.g., men and women), calculate the combinations for each category separately and multiply them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 7: Selecting from Multiple Categories**
> A committee of $5$ people is to be selected from $6$ men and $7$ women. How many committees can be formed if there must be exactly $3$ men and $2$ women?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Select the men: Choose $3$ men from $6$, which is $^{6}C_{3}$.
> 2. Select the women: Choose $2$ women from $7$, which is $^{7}C_{2}$.
> 3. Multiply the combinations since both selections must occur simultaneously:
>    $$ ^{6}C_{3} \times ^{7}C_{2} = 20 \times 21 = 420 $$
> 4. There are $420$ possible committees.

### 2. "At Least" or "At Most" Restrictions
For statements containing "at least" or "at most", you can sum the combinations of all valid scenarios. Alternatively, use the **complement method** by subtracting the unwanted scenarios from the total unrestricted combinations.

$$ \text{Favorable Selections} = \text{Total Unrestricted Selections} - \text{Unwanted Selections} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 8: Using the Complement Method**
> From the same group of $6$ men and $7$ women, how many committees of $5$ people contain at least $1$ man?
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Identify the complement: The opposite of "at least $1$ man" is "exactly $0$ men" (a committee of only women).
> 2. Calculate the total unconstrained committees: choose $5$ from $13$ people ($^{13}C_{5} = 1287$).
> 3. Calculate the committees with no men: choose $5$ from $7$ women ($^{7}C_{5} = 21$).
> 4. Subtract the all-women committees from the total: $1287 - 21 = 1266$.
> 5. There are $1266$ committees with at least one man.
